content.full_text:
April 30, 1979

Roberta Radcliff
819 Columbia Drive
Sacramento, California 95825

Dear Mrs. Radcliff:

Thank you for your letter dated April 15, 1979 in which you asked
about a registered lobbyist and spouse sending a wedding gift to a
consultant of a legislative committee.

It is unlawful for a lobbyist to make a gift to a legislative official
aggregating more than ten dollars ($10) in a calendar month or to act
as an agent or intermediary in the making of gifts, or to arrange
for the making of any gift, or to arrange for the making of any gift
by any other person (Government Code Section 86203).

However, the provisions of the Political Reform Act are not applicable
to the spouse of a lobbyist. The spouse may, without restriction, make
a gift to a legislative official if the lobbyist does not in any way
influence the spouse's choice of gift.

If a lobbyist and spouse jointly arrange for a wedding gift the value
of the gift cannot exceed twenty dollars ($20). The limitation is
based upon an opinion requested by Assemblyman Art Torres 2 PPPC
opinion 31 (No. 75-163, February 4, 1976) in which the Commission held
that the Assemblyman's interest was one-half of the value of the gift
wedding gift.

If I can be of further assistance, please contact me.

Sincerely,

Richard Eichman
Accounting Specialist
Technical Assistance and
Analysis Division

RE:vh

[illegible]

O O

F P R O
APR 15 12:12PM '79

April 15, 1979


Mr. Rich Eichman
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, Ca. 95814

Dear Mr. Eichman,

     I wanted to thank you for your quick reply
to a question I posed to you by telephone last week.
I would appreciate receiving a written reply to the
interpretation of FPPC regulations I requested by
telephone.

     My husband is a lobbyist. A close friend of
both of us is to be married. This person is a consultant
to a legislative committee. We would naturally like to
send a gift. How does this situation fall under FPPC
regulations?

Thanks for your help,


                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Roberta Ratcliff
                                        819 Columbia Drive
                                        Sacramento, CA 95825

State of California

Memorandum

To : PARTICIPANTS OF THE OPINION REQUEST Date : April 25, 1979
MEETING


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
RICH EICHMAN

Subject: Gifts From Lobbyists


FACTS:

A registered lobbyist makes or arranges for a twenty dollar ($20)
wedding gift to a legislative official and spouse.


QUESTION:

Is the value of the gift to the legislative official ten dollars
($10) or twenty dollars ($20)?


NOTE: The only reference I could find on the matter was an
opinion requested by Assemblyman Art Torres 2 FPPC Opinion
31 (No. 75-163, February, 1976).

"When the gift is the property of both spouses we
interpret the disclosure requirements for gifts
aggregating $25.00 or more in value to apply to
the officeholder's interest in the gift, not the
total value of the gift...the Assemblyman's interest
is one-half the value of the gift."


The participants of the opinion request meeting agreed that the
value of the gift to the legislative official is ten dollars ($10).
April 30, 1979

sections.question: A registered lobbyist and spouse want to send a wedding gift to a consultant of a legislative committee. How does this situation fall under FPPC regulations? Specifically, if a lobbyist and spouse jointly arrange for a wedding gift, what is the value limitation?
sections.conclusion: It is unlawful for a lobbyist to make a gift to a legislative official aggregating more than ten dollars ($10) in a calendar month or to act as an agent or intermediary in making a gift. However, the spouse of a lobbyist may make gifts without restriction if the lobbyist does not influence the choice. If a lobbyist and spouse jointly arrange for a wedding gift, the value cannot exceed twenty dollars ($20), with the lobbyist's interest being limited to ten dollars ($10) based on prior FPPC opinion 75-163.
sections.question_synthetic: A registered lobbyist and spouse seek guidance on the legality and value limitations of sending a wedding gift to a legislative consultant who is a close friend.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A lobbyist is limited to $10 gifts per month and cannot act as intermediary, but the spouse can give gifts freely if the lobbyist doesn't influence the choice. If they jointly arrange a $20 wedding gift, only $10 counts toward the lobbyist's limitation.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the original incoming request from Radcliff (dated April 15) and FPPC's formal response (dated April 30), plus an internal FPPC memorandum from April 25. The response letter clearly addresses the question and provides explicit conclusions with regulatory citations.

QUESTION: A registered lobbyist and spouse want to send a wedding gift to a consultant of a legislative committee. How does this situation fall under FPPC regulations? Specifically, if a lobbyist and spouse jointly arrange for a wedding gift, what is the value limitation?

CONCLUSION: It is unlawful for a lobbyist to make a gift to a legislative official aggregating more than ten dollars ($10) in a calendar month or to act as an agent or intermediary in making a gift. However, the spouse of a lobbyist may make gifts without restriction if the lobbyist does not influence the choice. If a lobbyist and spouse jointly arrange for a wedding gift, the value cannot exceed twenty dollars ($20), with the lobbyist's interest being limited to ten dollars ($10) based on prior FPPC opinion 75-163.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that a registered lobbyist and spouse may jointly send a $20 wedding gift to a legislative consultant, with the lobbyist's interest limited to $10 under gift restriction rules, while the spouse's portion is unrestricted.
